text.title: Coverage Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Coverage gaming, pragma abuse, async pitfalls, and parallel-run data loss patterns to avoid.

antipattern.description: Writing tests that execute code but contain no assertions (coverage gaming).
antipattern.reason: Lines are marked covered without any verification. Mutation testing (mutmut) reveals this: surviving mutants mean the code changed but tests still passed.

antipattern.description: Using # pragma: no cover on production business logic to hit a target.
antipattern.reason: pragma: no cover should only exclude generated code, abstract stubs, and unreachable defensive guards. Reviewers must check every pragma in code review.

antipattern.description: Measuring only repo total coverage instead of diff coverage on new code.
antipattern.reason: Legacy untested code drags total coverage down. Teams game the metric by writing trivial tests or marking exceptions. Diff coverage gates the thing the team controls: new code.

antipattern.description: Running pytest --cov without omit = ["*/tests/*"] in coverage config.
antipattern.reason: pytest-cov instruments test files themselves. Without omit, test lines inflate the total, hiding low coverage in production code.

antipattern.description: Using --cov-append without awareness in parallel pytest runs (pytest-xdist).
antipattern.reason: Each xdist worker writes a separate .coverage.N file. Without coverage combine, only the last worker's data is reported. Set parallel=True in .coveragerc and combine after the run.

rule.statement: Stop chasing 100% coverage. Set 80–90 as the practical ceiling; remaining 10–20% is defensive guards and error paths better validated by integration tests.
rule.rationale: Chasing 100% leads to testing trivial getters, mocking everything, and writing assertions that mirror implementation rather than verify behavior.
